questions.question: A researcher predicts that listening to classical music will improve test performance compared to silence. Which statement represents the null hypothesis?

options.A: Classical music improves test performance over silence
options.B: There is no difference in test performance between conditions
options.C: Silence leads to clearly higher test scores than music does
options.D: Music preference is the main confounding variable here

questions.explanation: The null hypothesis (H0) states that there is no effect or difference between conditions. A and C are directional alternative hypotheses (H1), and D names a methodological worry, not a hypothesis about the effect.

questions.question: In null-hypothesis testing, the significance level (alpha = .05) represents:

options.A: The probability of making a Type I error
options.B: The probability of making a Type II error
options.C: The measured effect size of the experiment
options.D: The probability that the null hypothesis is true

questions.explanation: Alpha is the false-positive rate: the probability of rejecting H0 when it is actually true (a Type I error). B describes beta (a Type II error), C confuses alpha with effect size, and D is wrong because a p-value or alpha never gives the probability that H0 is true.

questions.question: A researcher measures participants' natural levels of stress and correlates them with sleep quality. Why is this not a true behavioural experiment according to the lecture definition?

options.A: The study completely lacks any dependent variable to measure
options.B: Stress cannot be measured scientifically at all
options.C: There is no manipulation of an independent variable
options.D: The study does not take place in a laboratory

questions.explanation: An experiment requires manipulating an independent variable; here stress is only measured, making this correlational. A is false because sleep quality is a DV, B is false because stress can be measured, and D is wrong because a lab setting is not part of the definition.

questions.question: A researcher inserts several unrelated items between the critical memory items so participants do not notice the study's purpose. These unrelated items are called:
questions.explanation: Fillers are trials unrelated to the hypothesis that hide the manipulation and reduce strategic responding. A is what you manipulate, B is participants guessing the hypothesis, and C is a variable that systematically differs between conditions.

questions.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an experimental design from a correlational design?

options.A: An experiment establishes causality through manipulation
options.B: An experiment always uses a far larger participant sample
options.C: An experiment must always be run inside a proper laboratory
options.D: An experiment can only ever measure reaction-time outcomes

questions.explanation: By manipulating an IV and measuring its effect, experiments support causal claims, which correlational designs cannot. B is false because sample size is not the defining feature, C is false because location is irrelevant, and D is false because many DVs (memory, eye movements, decisions) are possible.

questions.question: A team compares ChatGPT users versus non-users on a writing task, using participants' existing usage to form the groups. How is this design best classified?

options.A: A true between-subjects experiment with random assignment
options.B: A purely correlational design with no grouping variable
options.C: A within-subjects experiment with counterbalancing of order
options.D: A quasi-experimental design based on naturalistic variation

questions.explanation: Groups are defined by a pre-existing, naturally varying characteristic rather than random assignment, which is the hallmark of a quasi-experiment. A is wrong because there is no manipulation or random assignment, B ignores the explicit grouping, and C is wrong because participants are not in all conditions.

questions.question: Which set of factors all increase the statistical power of an experiment?

options.A: Smaller sample, smaller effect, more variability, smaller alpha
options.B: Larger sample, bigger real effect, less variability, larger alpha
options.C: Larger sample, smaller real effect, more variability, smaller alpha
options.D: Smaller sample, bigger real effect, less variability, larger alpha

questions.explanation: Power (1 - beta) rises with a larger sample, a bigger true effect, lower variability, and a larger alpha. The other options each flip at least one of these factors in the wrong direction, which would reduce rather than increase power.

questions.question: A researcher runs a within-subjects study with two conditions, A and B, and worries about practice and fatigue effects. Is using counterbalancing the RIGHT or WRONG approach, and why?

options.A: WRONG — order effects can only be removed by switching to a between-subjects design
options.B: WRONG — counterbalancing changes the IV levels and so alters the hypothesis
options.C: RIGHT — splitting the sample so half do A then B and half do B then A cancels the average order effect
options.D: RIGHT — counterbalancing guarantees every individual participant shows zero order effect

questions.explanation: Counterbalancing (Group 1 A then B, Group 2 B then A) cancels the average order effect across the sample. A is wrong because counterbalancing handles order without dropping the within design, B misdescribes it, and D overstates it, since it balances the average effect, not each individual's.

questions.question: In a reading-time study, the critical words in one condition happen to be far more frequent than in the other. What does this represent?

options.A: A filler manipulation built into the materials
options.B: A confound that could explain the results
options.D: A measure of the study's effect size

questions.explanation: Word frequency systematically differs between conditions and could itself drive reading times, so it is a confound. A is wrong because fillers are unrelated trials, C is wrong because nothing is being balanced, and D confuses a nuisance variable with effect magnitude.

questions.question: A study compares three noise levels (silence, background, loud) using the same participants in all three, and reports only a p-value. Which critique is most justified?

options.A: An independent t-test should have been used for these three groups
options.B: A within-subjects design is invalid for three or more conditions
options.C: Three conditions require a correlational rather than experimental design
options.D: The p-value alone omits the effect size, which conveys magnitude

questions.explanation: A p-value indicates whether an effect is detectable but says nothing about its size, so reporting an effect size (e.g. eta-squared) is needed. A is wrong because three within-subjects conditions call for repeated-measures ANOVA, B is false, and C wrongly denies that a manipulated IV makes this experimental.
